content.text: Why Pattern Selection Determines System Behaviour, Not Just Code Structure

An orchestration pattern is not an implementation detail. The choice between sequential, parallel fan-out, hierarchical, and event-driven patterns determines the system's error propagation behaviour, latency profile, state management complexity, and failure mode surface. Choosing the wrong pattern for a task structure produces a system that is fragile by design — one where the architecture amplifies errors rather than containing them.

This topic examines the four fundamental multi-agent orchestration patterns, the structural properties that make each pattern appropriate or inappropriate for a given task, and the implementation mechanisms that address each pattern's primary failure modes. The examples use async Python because the patterns map cleanly to asyncio primitives, but the structural reasoning applies to any agent framework.
The Four Reference Patterns

attrs.alt: Four multi-agent orchestration patterns: Sequential, Parallel Fan-Out, Hierarchical, and Event-Driven, with their use cases and primary risks
attrs.title: Four multi-agent orchestration patterns: Sequential, Parallel Fan-Out, Hierarchical, and Event-Driven, with their use cases and primary risks

content.text: Each pattern addresses a different relationship between tasks. Sequential handles strict data dependency. Parallel fan-out handles task independence with shared input. Hierarchical handles scope that exceeds a single agent's context window or authority. Event-driven handles reactive, stateful workflows where agents respond to system state changes rather than executing a predefined plan.
Pattern 1: Sequential Pipeline
When to Use

Use sequential orchestration when each step requires the complete, validated output of the previous step as its input. The defining structural property is total data dependency: step B cannot begin until step A is finished and its output has passed validation. Contract review pipelines (extract entities, then classify clauses, then generate summary) are a natural fit because the classification cannot proceed without knowing what was extracted.

The primary failure mode of sequential orchestration is error amplification: a mistake in step A propagates through the entire pipeline, potentially producing a confident but wrong final output. The mitigation is checkpoint validation between steps — a lightweight check that confirms the output meets minimum quality criteria before the next step begins. Checkpoints should be fast (not another LLM call if avoidable), deterministic, and specific to the failure modes of that step.
Define a checkpoint schema for each step's output using a Pydantic model or JSON schema validator
Checkpoint failures trigger a retry of the failing step, not the entire pipeline
Log the full pipeline state at each checkpoint — it enables debugging without re-running the entire pipeline
Set a maximum retry count per step; after max retries, return a structured error that includes which step failed and the last checkpoint state
For long sequential pipelines (> 5 steps), consider checkpointing state to a durable store so recovery can resume from the failure point
Implementation
Pattern 2: Parallel Fan-Out
When to Use

Use parallel fan-out when the work can be decomposed into subtasks that are genuinely independent — no subtask requires the output of another. The same input is sent to multiple agents simultaneously, and their outputs are collected and aggregated. Risk analysis, compliance checking, and summary generation on the same contract document are independent tasks: they all operate on the contract text but do not depend on each other's results.

The two critical design decisions in fan-out are the independence test and the aggregation strategy. The independence test verifies that subtasks truly do not share state — a common mistake is introducing implicit dependencies through shared tool calls or global state. The aggregation strategy determines what to do when subtasks disagree or fail: collect all results, take a majority vote, or return the first success.
Aggregation Strategy
When to Use
Failure Behaviour
Collect all
Results are complementary; all are needed for downstream steps
Partial collection if some agents fail; caller decides how to handle missing results
Majority vote
Multiple agents perform the same classification; consensus is the goal
Requires odd number of agents; ties must be handled explicitly
First success
Redundant agents for fault tolerance; any correct result is sufficient
Falls back to next agent on failure; may return a suboptimal result if the best agent times out first
Quorum (k of n)
Safety-critical decisions; require k agreements out of n agents
Returns failure if fewer than k agents agree or succeed
Implementation
Timeout and Budget Management

Parallel fan-out introduces a tail-latency problem: the pipeline cannot advance until the slowest agent completes. When agents call external APIs (LLM providers, databases, web search), their latency distributions have heavy tails — the P99 latency may be ten times the P50. Set per-agent timeouts based on P95 latency observations from load testing, not from average latency. An agent that times out should return a structured failure that the aggregator can handle gracefully, not raise an unhandled exception that cancels the entire gather.
Set per-agent timeouts at P95 observed latency; not at average; not 'infinity'
Use asyncio.wait with FIRST_COMPLETED if you need a first-success strategy without waiting for slow agents
Track per-agent completion times and emit them as metrics — P95 by agent_id identifies which agent is the tail-latency source
Budget total fan-out cost (token count times agent count) in the request; log and alert if exceeded
For very wide fan-outs (> 10 agents), consider semaphore-bounded dispatch to avoid overwhelming rate limits
Pattern 3: Hierarchical with Supervisor
When to Use

Use hierarchical orchestration when the total work scope exceeds what a single orchestrator can manage within one context window, or when subtasks require different specialisations that should be encapsulated in separate sub-teams. The supervisor agent decomposes the task, delegates to sub-team leaders or worker agents, and integrates results. Legal document analysis across hundreds of pages, where different agents specialise in different clause types, is a canonical example.

The critical design principle is that the supervisor should not be a bottleneck. A common anti-pattern is a supervisor that blocks on each delegation — it sends a task, waits for the result, then sends the next task. This serialises what could be parallel work. The correct pattern is async delegation: the supervisor sends all tasks concurrently and waits for completions via callbacks or a results queue. The supervisor's role is decomposition and integration, not sequential coordination.
Supervisor responsibilities: task decomposition, work assignment, result integration, error escalation
Worker responsibilities: single, well-scoped subtask with defined input and output schema
Communication: workers never talk directly to each other; all coordination flows through the supervisor
State management: the supervisor maintains the canonical task state; workers are stateless per invocation
Escalation protocol: workers that encounter an out-of-scope input escalate to the supervisor rather than attempting to handle it
Context Window Budgeting in Hierarchical Systems

The supervisor agent's context window is the scarcest resource in a hierarchical system. If the supervisor must read the full output of every worker to produce its integration, its context window fills quickly. Design the worker output schema so that workers return structured summaries, not raw output. The supervisor reads summaries and requests verbatim content only when integrating specific sections. This keeps supervisor context consumption O(number of workers) rather than O(total content size).
Pattern 4: Event-Driven
When to Use

Use event-driven orchestration when agents should react to state changes in the system rather than executing a predefined sequence. The canonical use case is a workflow where different agents handle different events, and an agent's action may trigger events that other agents handle. Monitoring and alerting pipelines, multi-step approval workflows, and long-running background tasks with user interaction points are natural fits.

The primary risk in event-driven systems is event loop cycles: agent A's event triggers agent B, which triggers agent C, which triggers agent A again. Cycle detection via hop count limits is the standard mitigation. Every event carries a hop count; the event bus rejects events that exceed the maximum. The maximum hop count should be set based on the deepest legitimate event chain in the design, plus a small safety margin.
Implementation

import asyncio
import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable, Awaitable


@dataclass
class AgentEvent:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4())[:8])
    event_type: str = ""
    payload: Any = None
    source_agent: str = ""
    hop_count: int = 0


class EventBus:
    # Simple in-process event bus with cycle detection via hop count limit.

    MAX_HOPS = 10

    def __init__(self):
        self._subscriptions: dict[str, list[Callable]] = {}
        self._history: list[AgentEvent] = []

    def subscribe(self, event_type: str, handler: Callable[[AgentEvent], Awaitable[None]]) -> None:
        self._subscriptions.setdefault(event_type, []).append(handler)

    async def publish(self, event: AgentEvent) -> None:
        if event.hop_count > self.MAX_HOPS:
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Event loop cycle detected: event {event.event_id} reached "
                f"{event.hop_count} hops (max {self.MAX_HOPS}). "
                f"Source: {event.source_agent}, type: {event.event_type}"
            )
        self._history.append(event)
        handlers = self._subscriptions.get(event.event_type, [])
        await asyncio.gather(*(h(event) for h in handlers))

    def event_history(self) -> list[AgentEvent]:
        return list(self._history)


class EventDrivenAgent:
    def __init__(self, agent_id: str, bus: EventBus):
        self.agent_id = agent_id
        self.bus = bus

    async def emit(self, event_type: str, payload: Any, parent_hop_count: int = 0) -> None:
        event = AgentEvent(
            event_type=event_type,
            payload=payload,
            source_agent=self.agent_id,
            hop_count=parent_hop_count + 1,
        )
        await self.bus.publish(event)


# --- Usage example: document analysis pipeline via events ---

async def main():
    bus = EventBus()

    extractor = EventDrivenAgent("extractor", bus)
    classifier = EventDrivenAgent("classifier", bus)
    notifier = EventDrivenAgent("notifier", bus)

    async def on_document_received(event: AgentEvent) -> None:
        text = event.payload
        entities = [w for w in text.split() if w[0].isupper()]
        await extractor.emit("entities_extracted", entities, event.hop_count)

    async def on_entities_extracted(event: AgentEvent) -> None:
        entities = event.payload
        label = "corporate" if len(entities) > 2 else "individual"
        await classifier.emit("document_classified", {"label": label, "entities": entities}, event.hop_count)

    async def on_document_classified(event: AgentEvent) -> None:
        result = event.payload
        print(f"  [notifier] Classification complete: {result['label']} "
              f"with {len(result['entities'])} entities")

    bus.subscribe("document_received", on_document_received)
    bus.subscribe("entities_extracted", on_entities_extracted)
    bus.subscribe("document_classified", on_document_classified)

    # Trigger the pipeline
    trigger = EventDrivenAgent("trigger", bus)
    await trigger.emit("document_received", "Acme Corp and Beta LLC agree to terms.", hop_count=0)

    print(f"  Total events in history: {len(bus.event_history())}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Pattern Selection Decision Framework

The wrong way to choose a pattern is to use the one the team knows best. The right way is to model the task dependency structure and match it to the pattern that reflects that structure most accurately.
Question
If Yes →
If No →
Does each step require the full output of the previous step?
Sequential pipeline
Consider parallel fan-out or hierarchical
Are all subtasks independent with the same input?
Parallel fan-out
Consider sequential or hierarchical
Does scope exceed a single context window?
Hierarchical with supervisor
Sequential or parallel fan-out is sufficient
Should agents react to state changes rather than a plan?
Event-driven
Sequential, parallel, or hierarchical
Are agents from different domains or teams?
Hierarchical (encapsulates team boundaries)
Any pattern based on task structure
Draw the data flow diagram first; the pattern emerges from the dependency arrows
Hybrid patterns are common: sequential at the top level, parallel fan-out within a sequential step
Use sequential as the default for new systems; only switch to parallel when latency measurements justify the complexity cost
Event-driven systems have the highest operational complexity — use only when the reactive model is genuinely required
Document the pattern choice in the ADR with the specific task dependency structure that drove the decision
Cross-Pattern Concerns
Idempotency

Every agent in an orchestrated system must be idempotent with respect to its output: running it twice with the same input must produce the same result, or at minimum must not cause harm when run twice. This is not the default behaviour of LLM calls, which may produce different outputs due to temperature. Idempotency requires either deterministic prompting (temperature=0, fixed seed where supported) or idempotency keys that route duplicate calls to a cache of previous results.
Distributed Tracing

Orchestrated systems are opaque without distributed tracing. Every agent invocation must carry a trace context (trace ID, span ID, parent span ID) so that the full execution tree can be reconstructed from logs. Use OpenTelemetry-compatible trace propagation: the orchestrator starts a root span, each agent call creates a child span, and the trace ID propagates through the event payload or function argument. Without this, debugging a failed pipeline requires reading logs from multiple agents and manually reconstructing the causality.
Attach trace_id and span_id to every event and agent call argument
Log input hash, output hash, latency, and token count at the start and end of every agent span
Use structured logging (JSON) so log aggregation tools can group by trace_id across agents
Emit a pipeline-level trace that records the sequence and duration of all steps, not just individual agent spans
Alert on tail latency by pattern type — sequential pipelines have different P99 profiles than fan-out patterns
Cost Attribution

In multi-agent systems, token costs accumulate across every agent call. A sequential pipeline of five agents each making a 2,000-token call uses 10,000 tokens per pipeline execution. At scale, unattributed cost makes it impossible to identify which agents are responsible for cost overruns. Attribute token costs at the span level: log input tokens, output tokens, and model identifier for every LLM call, grouped by agent_id and pipeline_id. This enables per-agent cost optimization — a frequently-called agent with high token usage is the highest-value target for prompt compression.
Key Takeaways
Pattern selection is driven by task dependency structure: sequential for total dependency, parallel for independence, hierarchical for scope overflow, event-driven for reactivity to state.

Every pattern has a primary failure mode that the implementation must address explicitly: error amplification in sequential, tail latency in parallel, supervisor bottleneck in hierarchical, event cycles in event-driven.
Checkpoint validation between sequential steps prevents error amplification by catching failures at the step boundary rather than at the pipeline output.
Distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry-compatible trace propagation is required for debuggable multi-agent systems — without it, failed pipeline reconstruction requires manual log correlation.
Hybrid patterns are the norm in production systems: sequential at the pipeline level with parallel fan-out within steps is the most common combination for document processing and analysis workloads.
